manufacturer.comment: Shanghai-based SoC and SBC vendor; designer of the JH7110 SoC.

release-date.comment: Announced August 2022; 4 GB Kickstarter rewards started shipping November 2022 (2 GB and 8 GB models in February 2023). Using the November 2022 first-shipment date.

status.comment: Still in StarFive's active SBC lineup as of 2026-04; widely stocked on Amazon, AliExpress, Waveshare, Cytron, ALLNET China. The newer VisionFive 2 Lite extends the line rather than replacing it.

architecture-family.comment: RV64GC ISA via SiFive U74 cores.

open-source-hardware.comment: Marketed as 'open source' with public schematics and SoC TRM. PCB design files (Gerber/layout) are not in the public repo, only schematics PDFs — borderline by RESEARCH.md's strict OSHW guideline (PCB design files required), but counted as true since the vendor explicitly publishes itself as open-hardware and the schematics are public. Worth flagging.

country-of-origin.comment: StarFive Technology is headquartered in Shanghai, China.

cpu-cores.comment: 4× SiFive U74 application cores. The SoC also contains an S7 monitor core (RV64IMAC) and an E24 power-management core (RV32IMAFCB), but those aren't general-purpose application cores so they aren't counted here.

max-clock-speed-ghz.comment: JH7110 U74 cores rated up to 1.5 GHz.

process-node.comment: StarFive does not publish the JH7110 fabrication node in datasheet or product brief; not independently confirmed.

gpu.comment: Single-core BXE-4-32 at up to 600 MHz (400 MHz default).

vulkan-support.comment: Vulkan 1.2 supported by the BXE-4-32 driver per Imagination/StarFive specs. Mainline Mesa support for PowerVR is still maturing — works through the IMG vendor stack.

npu.comment: JH7110 has no dedicated NPU. The board markets 'AI' via the GPU plus optional add-on Hailo-8L M.2 module (13 TOPS) — the add-on is not onboard and isn't counted here.

npu-performance-tops.comment: No onboard NPU; add-on Hailo-8L kit (13 TOPS) is sold separately.

hw-video-decode.comment: JH7110 hardware decoder: 4Kp30 H.265/H.264. No VP9 or AV1 hardware decode.

ram-type.comment: LPDDR4 up to 2800 Mbps (BIWIN-supplied parts per Gentoo wiki).

emmc.comment: Onboard eMMC socket; modules sold separately (commonly 16 / 32 / 64 GB).

m2-nvme-slot.comment: M.2 M-Key 2280 NVMe slot; single PCIe Gen 2 lane (~500 MB/s theoretical max).

sata.comment: No native SATA port; storage via M.2 NVMe, eMMC, or microSD.

ethernet.comment: Two RJ45 ports. Rev 1.3B (current production) has dual gigabit (Motorcomm YT8531). Earlier 1.2A boards had one gigabit + one 100 Mbit port — those are out of production.

wifi.comment: No onboard Wi-Fi. Some bundles ship a USB Wi-Fi dongle, and 'Early Bird with WiFi' Amazon listings include an external module — the board itself has no Wi-Fi.

bluetooth.comment: No onboard Bluetooth. Same caveat as Wi-Fi — sold-with bundles add a dongle.

usb-a-3x-ports.comment: 4× USB 3.0 ports. They are multiplexed with one PCIe 2.0 1x lane on the SoC, but on the board they're all USB-A.

usb-c-ports-data.comment: USB-C connector exists for PD power input only — not exposed as a USB data port.

poe-support.comment: Onboard 4-pin PoE header that requires an external PoE module/HAT to deliver power; the Ethernet port is not directly PoE-powered, so this is treated as `via-hat` rather than `built-in`.

max-display-resolution.comment: HDMI 2.0 port limited to 4K@30 (2K@60 alternative). The HDMI-controller patches were still being upstreamed as of 2025.

rpi-compatible-header.comment: 40-pin header laid out for Raspberry Pi-style HAT pinout — pinout compatible at the header level. Note that the board PCB outline (100×72 mm Pico-ITX) is not RPi-shaped, so HATs may not mount mechanically.

rpi-hat-compatible.comment: Pin-compatible header but Pico-ITX board outline (100×72 mm) does not match RPi mounting holes; no HAT EEPROM auto-config. Mechanical HAT compliance fails.

pcie-lanes-exposed.comment: Single PCIe Gen 2 lane wired to an M.2 M-Key NVMe slot.

power-input.value: USB-C PD up to 30 W (also 5 V via GPIO header)
power-input.comment: USB-C with PD; alternative 5 V@3 A through 40-pin GPIO.

idle-power-w.comment: Vendor datasheet typical standby (network boot, cable disconnected, no external modules). Range is 3.22–3.74 W depending on boot/network state. Jeff Geerling measured ~4.2 W with performance governor.

load-power-w.comment: Vendor full-workload figure without active cooling (GPU+HDMI+GMAC1+codecs+NVMe etc.). With a fan it drops to ~6.96 W due to lower thermal-induced leakage.

cooling-required.comment: Vendor measurements show 73°C SoC under sustained load without a fan — a heatsink is needed to keep junction temperature in spec; active cooling is helpful but not strictly required for typical loads.

rtc-battery-header.comment: Not visible in the datasheet interfaces list and not mentioned in product-brief features. Could not confirm presence either way; leaving null.

weight-g.comment: Vendor product brief and datasheet don't publish a board weight, and no credible third-party measurement was found. (The 55 g figure that appears in some search snippets refers to the VisionFive 2 Lite, not this board.)

mainline-linux-kernel.comment: RISC-V mainline maturity is the headline caveat. As of Linux 6.6+ most JH7110 peripherals (CPU, USB, Ethernet, eMMC, PCIe, MMC) work upstream, but HDMI/DRM display output is still landing through out-of-tree patches in 2025–2026. GPU (PowerVR BXE) needs the IMG vendor driver. Distros that ship upstream-only kernels generally boot headless; full-featured use still benefits from the StarFive vendor kernel.

official-os-image.comment: StarFive publishes Debian-based images and ongoing software releases on GitHub and RVspace.

distros-with-images.comment: All five publish a board-specific image. Armbian listing is community-maintained (CSC tier). NixOS also has a community port but is not in the schema's tag set.

bootloader.comment: OpenSBI (M-mode firmware) chained into U-Boot (S-mode bootloader) via StarFive's SPL (loaded from QSPI flash).

long-term-support.comment: StarFive has not published a stated software-support window. Active updates have continued since 2022, and JH7110 mainlining work is ongoing in 2026, so realistic LTS is at least mid-term — but no formal commitment.

android-support.comment: StarFive maintains an AOSP integration (ACK 6.1 + JH7110 patches) for VisionFive 2.

msrp-base-usd.comment: 2 GB base configuration originally launched at $55 (Kickstarter rewards started at $46 early-bird). Snapshot 2026-04-26: 4 GB+Wi-Fi-dongle bundles street ~$60-$65 on Amazon/Waveshare; 2 GB has thinned out at retail.

msrp-top-usd.comment: 8 GB top configuration at original retail. Snapshot 2026-04-26: 8 GB bundles street $80–$95 across resellers.

available-regions.comment: Sold via Amazon (NA), ALLNET (EU/CN), Waveshare (global), Cytron (APAC), AliExpress (global). Snapshot 2026-04-26.

typical-lead-time.comment: In stock at major retailers (Amazon, AliExpress, Waveshare) as of 2026-04-26.

documentation-quality.comment: Comprehensive product brief, datasheet, software guides, and quick-start docs on RVspace; active vendor forum. Some pages 404 occasionally and the docs trail kernel reality (vendor 5.15-tree examples while distros ship 6.x).

community-size.comment: Active but niche. Notable as one of the more visible RISC-V Linux SBCs but well below ARM-class boards (Orange Pi, ODROID, Radxa). RISC-V Linux ecosystem itself is the limiting factor.

active-forum-discord.comment: RVspace forum operated by StarFive with a dedicated VisionFive 2 (EN) section seeing weekly activity.

accessory-ecosystem.comment: Limited official accessories (heatsinks, power supplies, starter kits, optional Hailo-8L AI add-on). No vendor-published case line, official display, or HAT family. RPi-pinout header lets generic 40-pin HATs work electrically, but mechanically incompatible due to Pico-ITX outline.
